title: The Sleep Solution: Why Your Sleep Is Broken and How to Fix It
rationale: The Sleep Solution fills an essential gap in the sleep-book landscape: where Why We Sleep provides laboratory science, Winter provides clinical practice. Written by a board-certified neurologist and sleep medicine specialist with 20+ years of patient encounters, this book reframes insomnia as a conditioned behavioral and cognitive problem rather than a sleep-production failure. It popularized CBT-I for a general audience and established the clinically grounded counterpoint to the alarmist narrative of contemporary sleep literature. Its unique contribution is the sleepiness-vs-fatigue distinction, the myth-busting opening, and a practical self-directed CBT-I program that can be implemented without a therapist.
excerpt: A neurologist and sleep medicine specialist dismantles the myths about sleep and offers an accessible, clinically grounded framework for understanding and curing insomnia and other sleep problems without pills.
keyIdeas: Sleep problems are less 'not sleeping' than 'poor quality sleep' — and insomnia is as much about fear and frustration as about wakefulness
The two-process model (circadian + homeostatic) governs sleep; optimizing both beats drug-based approaches
Sleep hygiene, CBT-I principles, and simple behavioral changes can resolve most insomnia without pills
keyTakeaways: Set and hold a consistent wake-up time — it is more powerful than a bedtime
The bedroom should be reserved for sleep and sex only; phones, TVs, and laptops are seductive sleep thieves
Insomnia is largely a conditioned response: anxiety about not sleeping fuels the problem; CBT-I breaks that cycle
whoShouldRead: Chronic insomniacs who have tried pills, apps, and advice with little success
Healthcare providers seeking a clinically grounded introduction to sleep medicine
Anyone confused by conflicting sleep advice who wants a science-based framework
Co-sleepers and couples where one partner's habits disrupt the other's rest
whoShouldSkip: Researchers seeking detailed neurobiology of REM and NREM mechanisms — Winter covers this lightly and clinically
Readers who want a one-size-fits-all prescription for how many hours to sleep
Anyone looking for nutritional or supplement-heavy protocols
